beats.prompt: Warm-up. Pick one item at random from a group. Match the group size to your chance of grabbing the single best one.

feedback.correct: Exactly — a blind pick of 1 from n lands the best with probability 1/n. Hold that number; it's the bar to beat.
hints: Each item is equally likely to be the best, and you pick one — so the chance is 1 over the group size.
1 of 2 → 1/2; 1 of 3 → 1/3; the bigger the group, the smaller the chance.

beats.prompt: Three candidates interview in a random order. You must hire or pass the moment each finishes — no callbacks. If you ALWAYS hire the first candidate, how often do you end up with the best of the three?

1/3.note: Right. The best candidate is equally likely to be 1st, 2nd, or 3rd. 'Hire the first' wins only when the best happens to arrive first — that's 1 case in 3.

1/2.note: Tempting, but hiring is not a coin flip. The best is in one of 3 equally likely positions; 'hire first' catches it only when it lands 1st — that's 1/3, not 1/2.

2/3.note: Too high — 'hire first' ignores everyone after position 1, so it can only win when the best arrives first: 1 in 3.

beats.prompt: The rules of the game, before we play.

interaction.title: What makes this hard
interaction.body: You meet candidates one at a time in a random order. After each one you know only how they RANK against those you've already seen — not their absolute score. You must accept or reject on the spot, and a rejection is final: no going back. You win only if you end up holding the single best of them all. That last rule — irrevocable choice — is what turns an easy ranking into a genuine puzzle.

feedback.correct: Keep those three constraints in mind: random order, relative ranks only, no take-backs.
hints: You never see a candidate's true score — only how they compare to earlier ones.
Every rejection is permanent; you can't recall someone you passed on.
Read it, then tap Continue.

beats.prompt: Watch 'hire the first' play out. The bars are the candidates' true quality (taller = better), revealed in arrival order. The ★ marks the actual best.

feedback.correct: 'Hire the first' grabbed candidate #1 and walked away — never seeing that the best (★) was right behind. With no look-ahead, you're just guessing.
hints: Cutoff 1 means you accept candidate #1 no matter what.
Candidate #1 here is only the 2nd-best; the best arrives at position 2 — too late.
Taking the first locks you in before you've seen the field.

beats.prompt: Same 'hire the first' rule, a new random order — this time the best happens to arrive first. Drag the cutoff to feel how little control you have when you never look ahead.

feedback.correct: A win — but only because the best landed in seat 1. 'Hire the first' wins exactly when the best is first, and that's 1 time in 3.
hints: With cutoff 1 you always take whoever is first; you win iff that person is the best.
The best is equally likely to be in any of the 3 seats.
So 'hire first' succeeds 1/3 of the time — no better than a blind pick.

beats.prompt: Make it exact. List the 3 candidates in every possible arrival order. In how many of those orders does the best arrive FIRST — and so, what is 'hire the first' chance of winning?

fields.label: Orders (out of 6) where the best is first

feedback.correct: There are 3! = 6 orders; in 2 of them the best is first (the other two slots can fill in 2! = 2 ways), so the chance is 2/6 = 1/3 — matching the blind pick exactly.
hints: Three candidates have 3! = 6 equally likely arrival orders.
Fix the best in seat 1; the remaining two fill seats 2–3 in 2! = 2 ways.
2 winning orders out of 6 → 2/6 = 1/3.

beats.interviewNote: The interviewer's setup: n candidates, random order, relative ranks only, irrevocable accept/reject, win only on the single best. The key insight to state out loud is that ANY rule ignoring the candidates seen so far — take the first, take the last, take a fixed seat, take one at random — wins with probability exactly 1/n, because position of the best is uniform. Beating 1/n REQUIRES using the information from earlier candidates. That observation is the whole reason the threshold strategy (next lesson) exists.
beats.prompt: Three 'no-information' strategies, one number. Reveal how each does at finding the best of n.

lenses.label: Take the first
lenses.body: Commit to candidate #1. Wins iff the best is in seat 1 → 1/n.

lenses.label: Take the last
lenses.body: Wait, then must take candidate #n. Wins iff the best is in seat n → 1/n.

lenses.label: Take a random seat
lenses.body: Pick a seat in advance, hire whoever lands there. Wins iff the best is there → 1/n.

feedback.correct: All three tie at 1/n. None of them LOOKS at the candidates — and that's exactly why they can't beat a blind guess.
hints: Each strategy commits to a position without using what it sees.
The best is equally likely to be in any single position, so any one fixed position wins 1/n.
To do better, a rule must react to the candidates it has already seen.

beats.prompt: Raise the stakes: 10 candidates now. What is 'hire the first' chance of landing the very best?

feedback.correct: 1/10 — and with 100 candidates it's 1/100. The more there are, the worse a blind commitment gets. We need a rule that uses information.
hints: Same logic as before: the best is equally likely to be in any of the 10 seats.
'Hire first' wins only when the best is in seat 1.
That's 1 in 10 = 1/10.

interaction.scenario: You will interview 4 candidates in random order and, stubbornly, decide in advance to hire the 2nd person you see — ignoring everyone else. (a) What is your chance of getting the best of the 4? (b) Could ANY rule that fixes a single seat in advance beat \(\frac{1}{4}\)?

feedback.correct: \[ P(\text{best in seat 2}) = \frac{1}{4} \] The best candidate is equally likely to occupy any of the 4 seats, so committing to seat 2 wins exactly \(\frac{1}{4}\) of the time. The same holds for seat 1, 3, or 4 — every fixed-seat rule wins \(\frac{1}{n} = \frac{1}{4}\). No fixed seat can beat \(\frac{1}{4}\); to do better you must USE the candidates you've already seen. That's the door we open next.
hints: The best candidate's seat is uniform over the 4 positions.
Hiring a fixed seat wins exactly when the best lands in it: \(\frac{1}{4}\).
Every fixed seat gives \(\frac{1}{4}\), so none beats it — answers: \(\frac{1}{4}\), no.

feedback.correct: Irrevocable choice caps every blind rule at 1/n. The only way up is to spend the early candidates as scouts. Next: Look, Then Leap — the strategy that smashes 1/n.
hints: No-information rules all tie at 1/n.
Information from early candidates is the only lever.
Next lesson: reject a few on purpose, then pounce.
